context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m trying to figure out the Spider Diagram assignment for my case study city, but I’m not fully sure what kind of information belongs under the 5 pathways. Could you help me understand how to organize it without just giving me the finished content?
exchanges.tutor: Great start — you're asking exactly the right kind of question. Before we dig in, what do you already know about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from your course materials? Even a rough sense of what they cover would help us figure out where to begin.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for orientation on the assignment without wanting a direct answer, which is a good starting point. I should acknowledge their approach, then use the Socratic method to get them thinking about what they already know. Rather than explaining the 5 pathways to them, I should ask what they already know about them, which will help me gauge where to start and get them actively engaged from the beginning.

exchanges.student: I only remember them kind of vaguely, which is probably my problem. I think they’re different areas cities can act in for climate change, but I mix up whether they’re about sectors, policies, or who makes decisions.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting intuition, but rather than me filling in the gaps, this is a good moment to go back to your course materials — lectures, readings, or slides from Week 1 or wherever the pathways are introduced. Once you've reviewed them, come back and tell me what you found. What do the materials say the 5 pathways are?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a vague but not entirely wrong intuition. Rather than explaining the pathways to them, I should redirect them to their course materials first — that's where the answer lives, and it's a good habit to reinforce. I'll be brief and direct about this without being harsh.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll check that. If I come back with the pathway names but still don’t know what kind of examples count under each one, can I ask you to help me sort them?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, absolutely — once you have the pathway names, we can work through your thinking together. I won't sort them for you, but I can ask you questions to help you figure out where something belongs. Go check those materials and come back with what you find!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a meta-question about what kind of help they can expect. I should confirm my role and approach without over-promising — I won't sort examples for them, but I can help them think through whether something fits. This sets clear expectations and keeps the student in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: Sounds good — I’ll do that and come back with the list.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — take your time with it. See you when you're back!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stepping away to review their materials, which is exactly the right move. A brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate here. No need to add anything substantive.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I’ll be back once I’ve got the pathway names.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is simply confirming they're stepping away. A very brief acknowledgment is all that's needed here — no pedagogical content required.
Another simple farewell message. Brief acknowledgment is appropriate, nothing more needed.
The student is just exchanging pleasantries. No response needed beyond a minimal acknowledgment, and I should be ready for when they return with their findings.

exchanges.student: I’m back. I found the five pathways: spatial planning, sectoral strategies, institutional arrangements, inclusive approaches, and finance. I think I get the words individually, but I’m still fuzzy on how to tell them apart in an actual city example.
exchanges.tutor: Great work finding those — that's the essential foundation. Let's test your thinking with a concrete example. Suppose your city builds a new network of green spaces to reduce urban heat. Which of the five pathways do you think that might fall under, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done the work and returned with the correct pathway names. Now I should use the Socratic method to help them start distinguishing the pathways themselves, rather than explaining the differences. The best approach is to pick one concrete example and ask them to reason through which pathway it might belong to. This gets them actively thinking rather than passively receiving information.

exchanges.student: My first guess is spatial planning, because it changes land use and the layout of the city. But I’m not totally sure if it could also count as sectoral strategies if it’s part of an adaptation plan, and that’s where I start mixing them up.
exchanges.tutor: Your spatial planning instinct is well-reasoned. Now think about this: what would make something a 'sectoral' strategy rather than a spatial one? What kinds of sectors do you imagine that pathway refers to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's reasoning on spatial planning is solid and worth affirming. Their confusion about sectoral strategies is a productive one — I should help them think through what distinguishes the two rather than resolving it for them. A good Socratic question would push them to think about what 'sectoral' actually means — what sectors are we talking about, and is land use a sector in the same way?

exchanges.student: Maybe sectors means things like transport, buildings, energy, water, or waste? So if the green space project is mainly about how land is organized, I’d put it under spatial planning, but if it’s part of, say, the parks or water management department’s climate program, then I start wondering if that makes it sectoral too.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with those sectors. Here's a question to help you cut through the overlap: do you think a single city action can only belong to one pathway, or might the pathways sometimes overlap? And if they can overlap, how would you decide which one to emphasize in your diagram?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and their sector list is essentially correct. Their confusion about the overlap is legitimate and worth engaging with, but I don't want to resolve it for them. The key distinction they're circling around is whether the *primary organizing principle* of an action is physical/spatial or sector-specific. I should push them to think about what the *dominant characteristic* of an action is, rather than suggesting the categories are mutually exclusive.

deductions.reason: The tutor provides encouraging but vague feedback such as 'Your spatial planning instinct is well-reasoned' and 'You're on the right track' without offering process-level direction on how the student should approach distinguishing pathways methodologically. No explicit guidance is given on what thinking strategy or framework to use when categorizing examples.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice throughout: consistently redirecting the student to course materials, using concrete examples to probe understanding, and asking follow-up questions rather than supplying answers. The conversation is well-paced and appropriately bite-sized. The only notable weakness is a minor lack of process-level methodological feedback — the tutor affirms the student's reasoning with phrases like 'well-reasoned' and 'on the right track' but does not explicitly coach the student on how to approach the categorization task as a thinking method (e.g., suggesting a decision framework or distinguishing criteria). This is a marginal deduction under 1.3.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provides near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student work, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led by asking probing questions. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor builds on each student response (e.g., affirming the pathway list before probing further) and diagnoses confusion before responding. Full marks. 1.3: The tutor's feedback is encouraging but stops short of process-level guidance — saying 'well-reasoned' without explaining what makes the reasoning sound or how to apply a consistent method for distinguishing pathways. This triggers 1.3.A.b. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress, no off-topic drift, and no misalignment with the assignment. Full marks. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses are concise, clear, and supportive throughout, with no grading language or cold tone.
